Project Report · Computer Vision × Plant Biology

Plant Motion Tracking with locate-anything

Studying plant movement through point-based localization with an open-vocabulary vision-language model (LocateAnything-3B): an evaluation across two contrasting time-lapse datasets, a working pipeline, and measured results.

Date 17 July 2026 Model LocateAnything-3B · GGUF q8_0 (6.3 GB) Engine locate-anything.cpp (C++/ggml) Hardware Intel i5-12450H · CPU-only

SummaryThree findings

Finding 1 — The model works

On a color, side-view bean time-lapse, locate-anything detected the seedlings with 92–99% coverage and stable identity. An end-to-end tracking pipeline was built on top of it.

Finding 2 — But not on every dataset

On a grayscale, top-down circumnutation experiment, coverage was only 19–27%; on most frames the model detected the text labels burned into the video rather than the plants.

Finding 3 — No fast circumnutation

Sampled densely enough to see it (13 min/sample): no 1–3 h circumnutation was detected. Only a slow ~20–29 h, circadian-scale oscillation is present (coarse estimate).

IntroductionGoal and method

Circumnutation is the circular, oscillatory movement of growing plant organs. It is classically studied by following the position of a tip (apex) over time. The goal here was to automate that point-based localization with locate-anything — a vision-language model that detects objects from a text prompt — instead of hand-clicking every frame.

The general approach is the same on both datasets: feed the frame to the model, assign the returned boxes to plants, track each plant's point across frames, remove the growth drift, and analyse the remaining oscillation. The decisive difference is which image type the model can actually handle — and that is largely what this report is about.

Part A · Main resultBean seedling tracking

Color, side view — the image type locate-anything is good at.

Data

“Lima Bean Time Lapse” from Wikimedia Commons (David Marvin, CC BY 3.0): 3567 frames, 1280×720, color, side view, three bean seedlings against a dark background, filmed over six days with more than 1,600 photographs.

The video is not continuous plant footage: it opens with underground germination and ends with a logo card. A cheap 27-second green-mask scan — using no locate-anything compute at all — was used to find the usable range.

Pipeline

  1. bean_detect.py — full-frame locate-anything detection on every 5th frame, prompt seedling, fast mode. 265 frames at ~80 s/frame → ~3.8 hours of CPU compute.
  2. bean_track.py — assign boxes to the three plants via fixed x-bands → track the box center → reject outliers with a moving median → interpolate.
  3. bean_period.py — linear detrend (removes growth drift) → autocorrelation + FFT for the period.

Spending a cheap pre-scan to aim the expensive VLM at the right frames became a recurring design decision.

Detection performance

Dense run, 265 frames. Identity stability: the three plants stayed in separate x-bands (~293 / 573 / 959 px) with no swapping.
MetricValue
Frames with exactly 3 boxes155 / 169
Coverage — plant 199%
Coverage — plant 292%
Coverage — plant 399%
Outlier frames rejected6 / 7 / 1

Measured motion

Box center, detrended. The sparse (89-frame) and dense (265-frame) runs gave the same ranking and similar amplitudes — the amplitude measurement is independent of sampling density, i.e. robust.
PlantOscillation std-xPeak-to-peakTotal pathstd-x at 89 frames
118.1 px98.2 px1217 px16.7 px
210.1 px71.8 px882 px8.8 px
35.6 px29.2 px649 px5.4 px

Plant 1 is the most active and plant 3 the most static — roughly a 3× difference. Plant 1 shows a pronounced swing between frames 2700–2900 (x: 295→440→235).

Period analysis

Sampling every 5th frame gives 13 min/sample, with a resolvable range of 0.4 h (Nyquist) – 29 h. We were therefore comfortably able to see a 1–3 h circumnutation. The time scale is approximate (“6 days / 3268 frames” → ~2.6 min/frame); since the video may be edited, values in hours are not exact.

Dominant period via autocorrelation and FFT. Plant 2's 51–58 h in x equals the observation window (58 h) itself — an artifact, not a cycle.
PlantAutocorrelation (x)FFTInterpretation
126.9 h19.5 hcircadian scale
251.6 h (x) / 24.9 h (y)58.4 / 29.2 hx artifact, y circadian
3no peak29.2 hweak

Main finding — a robust negative result

No 1–3 h circumnutation was detected, and the sampling was strong enough to see it (13 min; Nyquist 26 min). The most likely objection was also tested and refuted: “the box center averages the whole plant and may be damping the tip's oscillation” → the apex was analyzed separately and produced identical periods (27.1 vs 26.9 h; FFT 19.5 h in both). So this is not damping; the fast motion is genuinely absent.

This is not “we couldn't measure it” but “we looked with sufficient resolution and it wasn't there” — a much stronger statement.

Secondary finding — coarse, weak evidence

All three plants show a slow ~20–29 h oscillation, which is the circadian scale (the video spans a 6 day/night cycle, so daily leaf movement is plausible). But no precise period can be given: the window contains only ~2 cycles, and the FFT's “19.5 h” and “29.2 h” are adjacent frequency bins (265/3 and 265/2 samples) — indistinguishable at this scale.

Why no circumnutation? Unknown. Possibilities: the seedlings may have passed their active circumnutation phase; the video may be edited with scenes joined together (which breaks a continuous time axis); the 2D projection of 3D circular motion in a side view may be weak.

Part B · Evaluationlocate-anything on grayscale, top-down data

Circumnutation Tracker sample data: 757 frames, 768×576, grayscale, top view, 16 seedlings, 5-minute interval.

Prompt and preprocessing attempts. Decode mode (hybrid/slow) did not improve coverage.
AttemptResult
Per-plant crop (incl. upscaling / CLAHE / contrast stretch)0 detections — the model never finds an isolated small plant in a crop
Full frame, bare small plant0–17 boxes per frame, inconsistent
Full frame, template Locate all the instances…: seedling.Better (frame 500: 1→15) but some frames still 0
Full frame plant / leaf / sprout26 boxes (the cap) — but it detects the text burned into the video
Validation against the hand-annotated ground truth, 6 frames.
GroupCoverageError (when found)
distilled water27%27.5 px
nutrient solution19%18.8 px

Why it fails on this data

locate-anything was trained on natural images (COCO-style: color, sharp, recognizable objects). This dataset is the opposite: grayscale, top-down, low contrast, and the target — a “growing tip” — is an abstract botanical concept. The model also mistakes the digits and labels burned into the video for objects. This finding is valuable in its own right because it maps the tool's limits of applicability.

Part C · ComparisonA classical computer-vision reference

Built on the same grayscale data to put locate-anything's performance in context — and it yielded the project's biological finding.

Method: crop a window around each plant's known origin → Otsu inverse threshold → track the centroid of the largest dark contour (the seedling body).

The two methods on the same grayscale dataset.
MethodCoverageErrorRuntime (757 frames × 8 plants)
locate-anything19–27%18–27 px~17 hours
Classical CV100%9.3 px10 seconds

Biological finding: the effect of nutrient solution

In the same video the top 8 seedlings were grown in distilled water and the bottom 8 in nutrient solution. This is the experiment's actual scientific question.

Post-germination, on the detrended oscillation. Both the automated CV and the human tracking agree in direction → a real effect, not a tracking artifact.
MetricDistilled (CV)Distilled (human)Nutrient (CV)Nutrient (human)
Period7.1 h6.2 h3.9 h3.9 h
Amplitude3.9 px3.1 px15.0 px21.5 px
Net rotation3.0 turns3.7 turns8.3 turns9.7 turns
Path length377 px314 px1589 px1996 px

Conclusion

Nutrient solution markedly accelerates circumnutation (period ~6–7 h → ~4 h) and increases amplitude and activity 5–7×. The finding is robust because two independent measurements — automated and manual — agree in direction.

AssessmentLimitations

  • The bean video is a growth time-lapse, not a controlled circumnutation experiment. Its signal mixes growth, nutation and leaf movement.
  • The time scale is approximate. The Commons description does not give the capture interval; a “6 days / 3268 frames” assumption yields ~2.6 min/frame. If the video is edited, values in hours shift — which is why periods are also reported in frames.
  • Period resolution is coarse. The 58-hour window contains only ~2 cycles; at this scale the FFT bins 19.5 h and 29.2 h are indistinguishable.
  • The grayscale dataset has n = 8 per group and no formal statistical test was run (though the effect is large).
  • Tracking of the nutrient group is slightly noisier (14.5 px vs 9.3 px), but the measured effect far exceeds this.

ImplementationTechnical notes

Three pitfalls were found and are commented in the code; they may recur in similar setups:

  • Non-ASCII path × CLI argument. The project path contained Masaüstü, so passing an absolute path to the CLI corrupted the “ü” via MSVC's ANSI argv and the file was not found. Fix: an absolute path for the executable (CreateProcessW is Unicode-safe), but relative ASCII paths for model/input/output plus cwd = repo.
  • Non-ASCII path × cv2.imwrite. OpenCV silently writes a corrupt file to an absolute ANSI path; the CLI then reads the broken frame and returns “0 detections”. The first 6-frame run returning zero was this bug's fault, not the model's. Fix: cv2.imencode(...)[1].tofile(path).
  • Cascading outlier rejection. Rejecting by distance to the previous point collapses once the plant drifts away with growth (86 of 87 frames were rejected). Fix: deviation from a local moving median (threshold 35 px, window 7) — this removes only isolated jumps.

AppendixFiles

Pipeline (Part A): bean_scan.py → bean_detect.py → bean_track.py → bean_period.py

Evaluation (Part B): la_detect.py, la_analyze.py, test_one_crop.py

Classical CV (Part C): ct_track.py, ct_analyze.py, ct_compare.py

Data: detections in data/det_boxes/*.json, tracks in data/bean_tracks.npz. Because the detections are committed, the analysis can be reproduced without repeating the 3.8 hours of compute.

Data attribution (required): “Lima Bean Time Lapse”, David Marvin, CC BY 3.0, via Wikimedia Commons.

Model: LocateAnything-3B (NVIDIA) · Engine: locate-anything.cpp, Ettore Di Giacinto & Richard Palethorpe, MIT.

Comparison dataset: Circumnutation Tracker sample set (Stolarz et al., Plant Methods 2014).
